briefing.incidentSummary:
NovaPay processes 40,000 transactions per day. For three weeks, micro-amounts between ₹1 and ₹9 have been silently siphoned from transactions — routed through dormant accounts and consolidated into a single external wallet at 03:00 AM every night.

A full antivirus scan returned clean. No malicious files found. No suspicious executables. No unauthorized scripts.

But the transfers kept happening.

The malware has no file on disk. It lives entirely in memory, hidden inside a legitimate running process.
briefing.missionText:
Your Mission:

› Identify the injected process thread with no on-disk binary

› Locate the RWX memory region used as a shellcode staging area

› Trace the covert C2 network channel to the external wallet

› Expose the API hooks intercepting transaction commit calls

› Collect at least 3 correct evidences

› Flag the right suspect

suspects.name: Aryan Mehta

digitalFootprint.locationTrace: Office — Server Room Badge Access confirmed

suspects.profileNotes: Deployed the last server patch 4 days before anomalous transactions began. The patch included an undocumented change to service account permissions — specifically allowing a new thread under svchost.exe to persist across reboots without triggering integrity checks. No legitimate reason for this permission change has been documented. CCTV footage near a hardware wallet store resolves to an individual matching Aryan's description. The patch was reviewed by a second engineer before deployment — their role remains an open thread.
suspects.description: Aryan Mehta is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Priya Nair
suspects.profileNotes: Authored the transaction processing module targeted by the API hook. Understands NtCommitTransaction deeply — theoretically capable of designing the hook. However, has no server-level deployment access. Cannot execute code on production infrastructure. Git commits from the relevant period are under review but show no suspicious changes.
suspects.description: Priya Nair is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Sanjay Kulkarni
suspects.profileNotes: Access is limited to the database layer. The injection operates at OS process level — outside Sanjay's access scope entirely. Memory evidence clears him: no DBA credential was used in any server-level operation during the breach window.
suspects.description: Sanjay Kulkarni is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

suspects.name: Unknown Insider / External Actor

digitalFootprint.locationTrace: External — VPS registered with prepaid card

suspects.profileNotes: The C2 server IP resolves to a VPS registered using a prepaid card via a cryptocurrency transaction chain. The final wallet address was purchased at a store near NovaPay's office. An external actor working with an internal collaborator cannot be ruled out — but the deployment access trail points inward.
suspects.description: Unknown Insider / External Actor is a person of interest in this case. Their motives and involvement remain under investigation.

items.label: svchost.exe PID 1024 — Orphan Thread — No On-Disk Binary
items.detail: The running process list from the transaction server shows svchost.exe appearing twice under PID 1024. The first instance has a legitimate on-disk binary at C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe. The second entry — thread 7 within the same PID — has no associated disk binary path at all: the field reads NONE. A legitimate svchost.exe thread always corresponds to a file on disk. A thread with no disk binary inside a trusted process is the primary indicator of process injection — malicious shellcode written directly into the process's memory space without ever being saved to disk. Standard antivirus tools scan files on disk; this technique evades them entirely.

minigame.title: Process Tree Inspector
minigame.hint: Review all running processes and their parent-child relationships. Flag any process thread that lacks a corresponding on-disk binary path.
minigame.instruction: Examine each process entry below. Select the PID and thread that has no associated on-disk binary file. Identify which parent process it is hiding inside.
hints: Look at the 'On-Disk Binary' column for each entry. A legitimate Windows system process always has a corresponding file path. Which entry says NONE?
The suspicious thread is inside svchost.exe — PID 1024. There are two entries for this PID. Compare them: one has a binary path, one does not.
The injected thread is thread 7 inside PID 1024 — it has no on-disk binary. Select PID 1024, thread 7 to confirm process injection.

onSuccess.message: INJECTION CONFIRMED — PID 1024 thread 7 has no on-disk binary. Malicious shellcode is hiding inside svchost.exe. Memory Dump panel now unlocked.

items.label: RWX Private Memory Region — 0x04A00000 — Shellcode Confirmed
items.detail: Analysis of the Virtual Address Descriptor (VAD) map for PID 1024 reveals a private memory region at 0x04A00000–0x04A60000 flagged with RWX (Read-Write-Execute) protection. This is highly abnormal. Legitimate processes almost never need a single memory region to be simultaneously writable and executable. RWX is the signature of injected shellcode: the attacker wrote malicious code into this private region (Write), then redirected the process's execution flow to run it (Execute). The region is Private with no mapped file — meaning it exists only in memory and has no corresponding disk location. This is exactly where a fileless payload lives.

minigame.title: Memory Region Analyser
minigame.hint: Scan the VAD map entries for PID 1024. Flag any private memory region with RWX protection flags — that combination is the shellcode fingerprint.
minigame.instruction: Review the VAD (Virtual Address Descriptor) map below. Identify and flag the memory region with abnormal protection flags that indicates injected shellcode.
hints: Look at the 'Protection Flags' column. Normal memory regions are R-X (code) or RW- (data). Which entry has all three: Read, Write, AND Execute?
The suspicious entry has protection flags 'RWX'. This means the region can be written to AND executed — the exact combination needed to inject and run shellcode.
The RWX region is at 0x04A00000. It is Private with no mapped file. Select this address to confirm the shellcode staging area.

onSuccess.message: SHELLCODE STAGING AREA IDENTIFIED — RWX private region at 0x04A00000 confirmed. Note this address — it will appear again in the API hook table. Network Connections panel now unlocked.

items.label: C2 Channel — PID 1024 — Outbound HTTPS — No On-Disk Binary
items.detail: Live network socket capture during the 03:00 AM activity window shows PID 1024 (svchost.exe) maintaining an outbound HTTPS connection to external IP 185.220.101.77:443. Critically, the owning process has no associated on-disk binary — confirming this connection originates from the injected thread, not from a legitimate svchost service. This is the command-and-control (C2) channel: the injected shellcode receives instructions from the external operator and exfiltrates micro-transaction proceeds through this encrypted tunnel. The destination IP resolves to a VPS in Eastern Europe registered using a prepaid card.

minigame.title: Network Socket Tracer
minigame.hint: Map each active network connection to its owning process and binary. Identify connections where the PID has no on-disk file — that is the C2 channel.
minigame.instruction: Review the active network connections below. Match each connection to its owning process. Flag the connection whose owning PID has no associated on-disk binary and is connecting to an external IP.
hints: Look at the 'On-Disk Binary' column for each connection. Which owning process has no binary on disk?
The suspicious connection belongs to PID 1024, thread 7 — the same injected thread you found in Phase 1. It has no on-disk binary and is connecting to an external IP.
The C2 channel is the outbound connection to 185.220.101.77:443. Select this connection to confirm the command-and-control channel.

onSuccess.message: C2 CHANNEL CONFIRMED — Injected thread inside PID 1024 is communicating with 185.220.101.77. Live exfiltration in progress. API Hook Table now unlocked.

items.label: NtCommitTransaction — HOOKED — Resolves to 0x04A00440
items.detail: Import Address Table (IAT) analysis of nova_txn.exe — the core transaction processing binary — reveals that NtCommitTransaction resolves to address 0x04A00440. The expected legitimate address is 0x7FFD4B20 (inside ntdll.dll). The actual resolved address 0x04A00440 falls inside the RWX private memory region identified at 0x04A00000 in Phase 2. This hook intercepts every transaction commit call: before the transaction is written to the database, the shellcode reads the amount, skims a micro-amount (₹1–9), routes it to a dormant staging account, and then passes the modified transaction forward. The database sees only the post-skim values — the manipulation is invisible at the DB layer.

items.label: NtQueryInformation — HOOKED — Resolves to 0x04A00520
items.detail: NtQueryInformation is also hooked — resolved address 0x04A00520, also inside the RWX region at 0x04A00000. This hook intercepts any process that queries the transaction totals, returning falsified values that hide the missing micro-amounts. When the operations team ran reconciliation reports, this hook ensured the numbers appeared balanced — concealing the ongoing theft from internal monitoring tools.

items.label: Correlation Matrix Brief
items.detail: Cross-case behaviour fragments were extracted from logs, commit metadata, and communication fingerprints. Match each fragment to the suspect it most strongly identifies.

hiddenItem.label: Hidden Suspect Unmasked
hiddenItem.detail: The matrix reveals a previously obscured operator profile tied to Aryan Mehta. Their unique workflow signature and language fingerprints align across all critical events.

minigame.title: Suspect Correlation Matrix
minigame.instruction: Match each forensic fragment to the most likely suspect. Only one mapping set is fully consistent.

fragments.label: Workflow Fingerprint
fragments.value: Task execution cadence matches an operator who batches actions in 17-minute windows and pivots immediately after privileged checks.
fragments.hint: Compare this to known operating patterns.
fragments.explanation: This disciplined pattern matches Aryan Mehta, who used the same cadence during the breach stages.

fragments.label: Noise Signature
fragments.value: An intentionally noisy decoy pattern appears in low-value logs with inconsistent timezone usage.
fragments.hint: This fragment points to a distractor suspect.
fragments.explanation: The inconsistent decoy style maps to Priya Nair and does not align with the core intrusion chain.

fragments.label: Language Marker
fragments.value: Incident notes reuse a rare phrase sequence that appears in insider coordination messages.
fragments.hint: Look for the suspect with matching communication style.
fragments.explanation: The phrase reuse is linked to Sanjay Kulkarni, but it appears only in support communications, not execution logs.

onSuccess.message: Hidden suspect profile unlocked: Aryan Mehta emerges as the only suspect matching the complete matrix.

items.label: Breach Window Interrogation Notes
items.detail: Interview transcripts captured each suspect's alibi during the critical breach window. Verify which alibi collapses against timeline telemetry.

hiddenItem.label: Critical Alibi Contradiction
hiddenItem.detail: Timeline reconstruction disproves Aryan Mehta's alibi. Their claimed location conflicts with system access records at the breach timestamp.

minigame.title: Breach Window Alibi Check
minigame.instruction: Review each alibi and identify the one contradicted by forensic timeline data.

timelineEvent.event: Privilege escalation and staged exfiltration occurred during this exact interval.

alibis.alibi: Claims to be offline in a routine maintenance meeting with no system access.
alibis.contradiction: Authentication and command logs show privileged actions from this suspect's account during the same window.

alibis.alibi: States they were in a monitored support call with screen recording enabled.
Reports compiling a non-production patch with signed CI traces.

onSuccess.message: Contradiction confirmed: Aryan Mehta's alibi collapses under timeline verification.

timeline.title: Malicious Server Patch Deployed
timeline.description: Aryan Mehta deploys a server patch including an undocumented service account permission change. Patch reviewed and approved by a second engineer.

timeline.title: First Anomalous Micro-Transfers
timeline.description: Operations team notices micro-amounts (₹1–9) routing through dormant accounts to an external wallet. Pattern repeats nightly.

timeline.title: Antivirus Scan — Clean
timeline.description: Full AV scan across transaction processing cluster. No malicious files found. Transfers continue.

timeline.title: Memory Forensics Investigation Begins
timeline.description: Experienced investigator suspects fileless attack. Memory image captured from SRV-TXN-01.
